outcome_contract.why: Staff need to record at checkout whether a guest used the fridge, and see that flag on the checkout screen so nothing is missed before the guest leaves.
outcome_contract.intended_outcome_statement: Staff can toggle a fridge-used flag per guest on the checkout screen; the FRIDGE column shows a fridge icon when the flag is set, so staff know to retrieve fridge items before the guest departs.

engineering_coverage.applicable: `Refrigerator` icon, toggle button disabled during write. Standard `Button` component with `disabled` prop — already supported by design system.
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: `Refrigerator` icon, instant visual flip. No disabled state needed.
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Icon + `Button` with `disabled` prop during async write. Simple, no extra state machine.

engineering_coverage.applicable: Empty: blank cell. Loaded/false: icon not shown, toggle button shown. Loaded/true: icon shown + toggle button shown. Pending: button disabled. Error: toast + button re-enabled.
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Same states minus pending/disabled. Adds: optimistic state + rollback state.
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Pending state managed via `isPending` per occupant in `Checkout.tsx` (a `Set<string>` of occupantIds currently writing). Passed to `CheckoutTable` alongside `onToggleFridge`.

engineering_coverage.applicable: Explicit `fridgeStorage` Firebase rule with role-gated write. Same for both options.
`console.error` + `showToast` on mutation failure. Same for both.
`isPending` logic is trivial to test — mock the mutation hook to not resolve and assert button is disabled.
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Rollback logic requires additional test cases for failure path.
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Fewer test paths to cover under Option A.

engineering_coverage.applicable: `Guest.fridgeUsed?: boolean` replaces `Guest.fridge?: string`. `CheckoutTableProps` gains `onToggleFridge` and `pendingFridgeOccupantIds?: Set<string>`. New schema, types, data hook, mutation hook. Same for both options (type contract is identical).
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Breaking type change on `Guest` interface — all callsites updated.

engineering_coverage.applicable: Identical Firebase write in both cases. Disabled button prevents double-fire without needing a debounce guard.
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Same Firebase write. Requires rollback on error, which is a second async state update.
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Option A is marginally more reliable (no rollback race condition).

engineering_coverage.applicable: No feature flag. Revert 4–5 files. Firebase node persists harmlessly. Same for both.

stage_payload.decision_summary: The checkout screen already has a FRIDGE column that always shows blank. All operator decisions are confirmed: data model is `{ used: boolean }` at `fridgeStorage/<occupantId>`, write path is an inline toggle per row, display is an icon. The implementation follows the bag-storage pattern exactly, extended with a direct-write mutation hook (bag storage is set via Prime; fridge is set directly from the checkout screen). The one design decision not fixed by the operator is how the toggle handles the async write window — this analysis resolves it.
goals: Staff toggle fridge-used per guest on the checkout screen.
FRIDGE column shows `Refrigerator` icon when `used === true`, blank otherwise.
Fridge flag is archived alongside the guest.
non_goals: No free-text, no item list, no POS transaction.
No fridge UI on any other screen.
constraints: Must follow bag-storage read pattern (Firebase node, Zod schema, data hook, `Checkout.tsx` integration).
Write path via new mutation hook using Firebase `update()`.
`Refrigerator` icon confirmed available in lucide-react installed in reception app.
assumptions: All authenticated staff roles may write (same as all other guest-facing mutations).

evaluation_criteria.Why it matters: Codebase coherence; reduces future maintenance cost
Staff must not be able to double-fire the toggle or get stuck in a broken state
Small internal tool; minimal code surface
No migration, no feature flag needed

options_considered.Description: Disable the toggle button while the Firebase `update()` is in flight. Local state: `isPending` boolean per row (lifted into `CheckoutTable` or managed in `Checkout.tsx` via a `Set` of pending occupantIds). On success: Firebase subscription delivers updated value → button re-enables with new state. On error: toast + re-enable.
options_considered.Upside: Simple. No optimistic-state rollback logic. Consistent with how the Complete button works (it already invokes async and implicitly blocks re-click via its callback). Matches existing patterns in the codebase.
options_considered.Downside: Visible latency: button is briefly greyed out. For a fast local Firebase write (< 200ms) this is barely perceptible.

options_considered.Description: Immediately flip `fridgeUsed` in local state before the Firebase `update()` returns. On error: roll back the local state + show toast.
options_considered.Downside: Adds rollback logic — a second state path. More code. Overkill for a < 200ms write to a local Firebase RTDB. The bag-storage display has no rollback logic and no issues reported.
options_considered.Key risks: Rollback failure leaves UI out of sync with Firebase.

chosen_approach.Why this wins: It requires no rollback logic. Firebase RTDB writes to a local node are fast enough that the disabled window is imperceptible. It is simpler to test — the pending state has exactly one success path and one error path, both trivially mockable. Option B's zero-latency advantage is not worth the rollback complexity for a < 200ms internal-tool write.
chosen_approach.What it depends on: Firebase RTDB being reachable (same dependency as the rest of the checkout screen — no new dependency introduced).

rejected_approaches: Option B (Optimistic update) — Rejected. The latency benefit is negligible for a < 200ms internal-tool write. The rollback code adds a second state path and additional test cases with no material UX improvement.

sequencing_constraints: Schema + types must exist before hook files (TypeScript import ordering).
`CheckoutTable.tsx` type change must land before `Checkout.tsx` wiring (TypeScript will catch mismatches).
Firebase rules update is independent and can land in the same commit.
Archive hook change is independent — can be a separate task or bundled.

areas.label: Fridge flag recording
areas.trigger: Guest hands fridge items to staff at checkout.
areas.future: 1. Staff opens checkout screen. 2. Guest row shows FRIDGE column with a toggle button. 3. Staff clicks toggle — button disables immediately. 4. Firebase `update()` writes `fridgeStorage/<occupantId> = { used: true }`. 5. Firebase subscription delivers update → `Refrigerator` icon appears; button re-enables. 6. Clicking again reverses: `used: false`, icon disappears.
areas.steady: Rest of checkout flow (loans, keycard, Complete button) unchanged.
areas.seams: Toggle must disable during write to prevent double-fire. Error path: toast + re-enable.

areas.label: FRIDGE column display
areas.seams: `Refrigerator` icon shown in FRIDGE cell when `fridgeUsed === true`; blank otherwise. Consistent with Luggage icon for bag storage.

areas.label: Guest archival
areas.future: `fridgeStorage/<occupantId>` read → copied to `archive/fridgeStorage/<occupantId>` → live path nulled.

areas.label: Guest deletion
areas.current: `useDeleteGuestFromBooking` nulls `bagStorage/<occupantId>` at line 119 but has no fridge equivalent.
areas.future: `fridgeStorage/<occupantId>` is nulled in the same `updates` map alongside `bagStorage`. Prevents orphaned fridge records.

areas.label: Firebase security
areas.future: Explicit `fridgeStorage` rule block: read for any authenticated user; write gated on staff/admin/manager/developer/owner role (same as all other guest-facing nodes).
areas.seams: Must deploy `database.rules.json` changes before or alongside code changes.
